raw_case:
Arteriovenous malformation associated with trophoblastic retention post-cesarean section: A case report and review


Introduction and importance

Uterine arteriovenous malformations are a rare but potentially life-threatening condition. They may be congenital or acquired and should be suspected in cases of severe or persistent uterine hemorrhage.

Case presentation

We present the clinical case of a 32-year-old woman who suffered from bleeding after a spontaneous miscarriage. Uterine arteriovenous malformation with trophoblastic retention was suspected on ultrasound and Doppler examination. Magnetic resonance imaging confirmed the diagnosis, leading to embolization of both uterine arteries, followed by operative hysteroscopy to remove the retained tissue, with a favorable outcome.

Clinical discussion

Uterine arteriovenous malformation is rare, with fewer than 100 cases reported in the literature (1). It is a potentially fatal condition due to the heavy bleeding that patients may experience. Color Doppler ultrasound (US) is a non-invasive method for initially diagnosing this rare condition, which can be confirmed by diagnostic angiography. A conservative approach or embolization is the preferred treatment to avoid hysterectomy in patients of childbearing age.

Conclusions

This case report emphasizes the use of ultrasound and MRI to diagnose a uterine AVM in a patient of childbearing age who presented with post-partum retention of products. It also showcases our experience with embolization in this patient, which allowed her to preserve her fertility.

Highlights

•Uterine AVM is rare entities in gynecology, but they represent a potentially life-threatening disorder.•Ultrasound with Doppler can raise suspicion for the diagnosis, while MRI clarifies any uncertainties.•Embolization has become the preferred treatment, replacing hysterectomy.1

Introduction

Uterine arteriovenous malformation (AVM) is a rare condition, with fewer than 100 cases documented in the literature [1]. It represents a major potential risk, as patients may be exposed to severe hemorrhage [2]. Color Doppler ultrasound (US) is an effective non-invasive method for the initial diagnosis of this pathology, which can be confirmed by diagnostic angiography. A conservative approach or embolization is a preferable treatment to avoid hysterectomy in patients of childbearing age [3]. This case report highlights our experience with a patient suffering from this rare gynecological pathology in our institution. This case was presented in accordance with the updated consensus Surgical Case Report (SCARE) guidelines [4].2

Case report

A 32-year-old G2P1 patient with no notable pathological antecedents, who underwent a cesarean section at term three years ago due to acute fetal distress, was admitted to our institution because of persistent low-grade metrorrhagia following a spontaneous miscarriage two months previously. She had initially been under observation for this bleeding, with a plasma beta HCG level measured at 13,500. The bleeding was considered to be due to trophoblastic retention, which led to her referral to our department for further treatment.Upon physical examination, the patient presented in good general condition, apyretic and hemodynamically stable, with no signs of anemia. A gynecological examination revealed a healthy cervix, with no vaginal lesions or active bleeding of endo-uterine origin.The plasma HCG assay was positive at 7100, slightly down on the previous test, with a hemoglobin level 12,1.Ultrasound combined with Doppler showed an anteverted and anteflexed uterus of normal morphology, measuring 50 (width) × 96 (height) × 44 (thickness) mm. The endometrium was thin and regular, with a heterogeneous image richly vascularised at the anterior isthmus over the cesarean scar, measuring 62 mm wide, 56 mm high, and 29 mm thick. Dilated vessels were observed, with veins showing velocities over 50 cm/s and low-resistance arteries with peaks over 100 cm/s (Fig. 1).A diagnosis of high-flow vascular malformations associated with trophoblastic retention on the isthmic cesarean scar was suspected. A pelvic Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) was then performed, confirming the presence of a retention measuring 30 mm associated with an AVM.Following a multidisciplinary consultation meeting, endovascular management was recommended, including embolization before evacuation of the retention by operative hysteroscopy.A right femoral arterial puncture was performed under local anesthetic with xylocaine and Risordan, followed by catheterization of the left uterine artery using a 5 French 135 cm BERN catheter. Arteriography revealed a large uterine arteriovenous formation, which was embolised with large (3/4) fragments of CURASPON, followed by a torpedo. The operation progressed favorably (Fig. 2).The patient underwent an operative hysteroscopy the same day to resect the trophoblastic retention. The operation revealed dehiscence of the anterior cesarean scar, with a retention of 3 to 4 cm long, partially necrotic and embedded in the scar. The operation was carried out without complications. Histopathological examination of the tissue revealed necrotic chorionic villi, confirming the diagnosis of an aborted pregnancy.The patient was followed up two weeks after the operation, reporting no bleeding, a negative beta HCG level, and an ultrasound showing regression of the AVM. At the 6-month check-up, she reported regular menstrual cycles with no abnormal uterine bleeding.3

Discussion

We present a rare case of acquired uterine AVM associated with trophoblastic retention, discovered after a miscarriage. The importance of this observation lies in the rarity of the cases reported and the originality of our diagnostic and therapeutic approach.Uterine AVM are rare entities in gynecology, but they represent a potentially life-threatening disorder, as patients may experience profuse metrorrhagia leading to hemodynamic instability. It is crucial to consider this differential diagnosis in women of childbearing age presenting with unexplained vaginal bleeding, as well as in post-menopausal women when an ultrasound scan reveals anechogenic structures [5]. Occasionally, these malformations may become apparent during exploration for pelvic pain, dyspareunia, or anemia [5].Uterine AVMs can be congenital or acquired [6]. Congenital AVMs are thought to arise from disruptions in embryological vascular development, leading to abnormal differentiation of capillaries and atypical connections between arteries and veins. These congenital forms may feature multiple vascular connections and can invade adjacent structures [7].Acquired AVMs are more prevalent and typically develop after uterine trauma, such as curettage, cesarean section, or pelvic surgery. The formation of abnormal connections between arteries and veins often occurs during the healing process, usually involving a single artery merging with a single vein. Additionally, acquired AVMs can be associated with infections, retained products of conception, gestational trophoblastic disease, and gynecological tumors [8].In cases of post-partum metrorrhagia, whether spontaneous or induced, the primary diagnosis to consider is often the retention, which may quickly necessitate uterine aspiration or curettage. However, in the presence of an AVM, this procedure can exacerbate the hemorrhage and pose a significant risk to the patient's life. Therefore, it is essential to highlight the importance of Doppler ultrasound in evaluating any instance of metrorrhagia to ensure that the possibility of an acquired AVM is not overlooked [9].Ultrasound, valued for its rapid, easy-to-perform, cost-effective, and non-invasive nature, has become the preferred technique for diagnosing arteriovenous AVMs. On greyscale ultrasound, uterine AVMs typically present as non-specific heterogeneous spaces within the myometrium, along with multiple cystic lesions and tubular anechoic areas. Common findings on color Doppler imaging include hypervascularization of the lesion, turbulent flow, and numerous tortuous feeding vessels, which may sometimes cause aliasing artifacts [10]. Spectral Doppler ultrasound reveals low values for both the resistance and pulsatility index, accompanied by high peak systolic velocity (PSV) flow. In normal myometrium, the PSV ranges from 9 to 44 cm/s, with a resistance index between 0.6 and 0.8, whereas in uterine AVMs, systolic and diastolic velocities can be 4 to 6 times higher [10].Studies have shown that PSV can be useful in distinguishing between safe and potentially dangerous AVMs [3]. A PSV of 0.83 m/s or greater is associated with malformations considered at risk, requiring immediate embolization. PSV values between 0.39 and 0.83 generally indicate a malformation of lesser concern, while values below 0.39 m/s are considered safe [3].Ultrasound is utilized to evaluate the effectiveness of embolization 24 hours post-procedure and to identify patients who may require repeat embolization. Additionally, it aids in differentiating between intrauterine retention, polyps, and endometrial hypertrophy [3].Digital subtraction angiography is considered the gold standard for diagnosing uterine AVM. This method demonstrates bilateral uterine artery hypertrophy and a hypertrophic, tortuous arterial mass with large feeding vessels. It confirms the diagnosis and identifies the main feeder vessels, thus guiding embolization [11].MRI is an effective method for diagnosing AVMs. When performed with a gadolinium-based contrast agent, MRI reveals predominantly arterial hypervascular flow and allows detailed assessment of vascular supply and drainage. Typical features of AVMs on MRI include a large uterus with a focal mass, junctional zone abnormalities, multiple areas of signal in the myometrium, intense enhancement on post-contrast images, and prominent parametrial vessels; however, disadvantages include high cost and limited availability in some regions [11].Three-dimensional tomography aims to determine the extent of the damage, exclude an ectopic lesion, distinguish congenital uterine AVMs from acquired ones, and identify the feeding vessels or uterine arteries [12].Computed tomography (CT) angiography can be used in patients who are unstable or have contraindications to MRI [13]. Although hysteroscopy can diagnose AVMs, its overall effectiveness is fairly limited [14].The strategy for treatment is primarily guided by the patient's hemodynamic status, the severity of the bleeding, age, desire for future fertility, potential for recurrence, and available treatment options.Initial management aims to stabilize the patient's hemodynamic status and stop any bleeding. Traditionally, hysterectomy was the preferred treatment, but the patient's desire to become pregnant is an essential consideration. Today, uterine embolization is the treatment of choice [15]. It has three main advantages: first, it allows for confirmation of the pre-treatment diagnosis during the arteriographic phase; second, it provides a quick and minimally invasive procedure; and third, it helps preserve the patient's obstetric prognosis. Selective embolization may be unilateral or bilateral, depending on the location of the feeding arteries. Various agents, such as coils, gelatin sponges, polyvinyl alcohol, detachable balloons, and isobutyl-2-cyanoacrylate, have been used for uterine artery embolization. The choice of agent depends on the size of the vessel to be embolized and whether temporary or permanent occlusion is required [16].Potential side effects may include mild fever, pain, infection, temporary lameness in the buttocks and lower limbs, peeling of the perineal skin, utero-vaginal and recto-vesico-vaginal fistulas, and bladder necrosis [17]. However, the risk of complications is extremely rare and significantly minimized when the procedure is conducted by a specialist in interventional radiology.It is important to note that embolization does not affect the menstrual cycle or pregnancy [17].Hysterectomy remains the treatment of choice for postmenopausal women with AVMs, for women who do not wish to preserve their fertility, as an emergency procedure in life-threatening conditions, or if transcatheter embolization fails [18]. Other surgical methods rarely used include hysteroscopic coagulation, laparoscopic coagulation, surgical ablation, and uterine artery ligation.In stable patients that can be closely monitored, a long-term conservative approach may be appropriate. Timmerman et al. reported that six out of ten cases of uterine AVMs resolved spontaneously, and oral contraception and methylergonovine maleate, administered first intramuscularly and then orally, are associated with regression of lesions [6].4

Conclusion

Arteriovenous malformations are rare and should be considered in cases of persistent bleeding in women of childbearing age with a history of spontaneous abortion, whether or not they have undergone uterine curettage. This awareness can help avoid interventions that may exacerbate the condition and facilitate timely, appropriate management. Ultrasound with Doppler can raise suspicion for the diagnosis, while MRI clarifies any uncertainties from ultrasound and offers a precise topographical diagnosis. Embolization has become the preferred treatment, replacing hysterectomy, which was previously the standard approach.

Abbreviations


AVMarteriovenous malformation


Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal.

Ethical approval

It is not necessary to obtain specific ethical approval from the Mohamed V Military Hospital for this situation. The case report does not contain any personal information.

Sources of funding

None.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Houssine Oukili, Meryem Benmoussa and Abdelhamid Benlghazi were responsible for the patient's diagnosis and clinical management. Abdelhamid Benlghazi and Houssine Oukili wrote the manuscript. Moulay Mehdi Elhassani and Jaouad Kouach contributed to the analysis, supervision, writing, reviewing, and editing of the manuscript for intellectual content. All authors have read and approved the final manuscript.

Guarantor

The corresponding author is the guarantor of submission.

Research registration number

The name of the registry: researchregistry.com/The hyperlink to the registration: https://www.researchregistry.com/browse-the-registry#home/The unique identifying number of the study: researchregistry

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare no conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 32-year-old female, G2P1.
- **Chief Complaint:** Persistent low-grade metrorrhagia following a spontaneous miscarriage two months prior.
- **History of Present Illness:** Persistent bleeding post-miscarriage; initial plasma beta-HCG level of 13,500; suspected trophoblastic retention; referred for further management.
- **Past Medical History:** Cesarean section three years ago due to acute fetal distress.
- **Personal History:** None mentioned.
- **Family History:** None mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Good general condition, apyretic, hemodynamically stable, no signs of anemia; healthy cervix with no vaginal lesions or active bleeding of endo-uterine origin.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Plasma beta-HCG: Positive at 7100, slightly reduced from prior.
  - Hemoglobin: 12.1 g/dL.
  - Ultrasound and Doppler: Heterogeneous vascularized image at anterior isthmus over cesarean scar; high-velocity dilated vessels.
  - MRI: Retention measuring 30 mm associated with uterine AVM.
  - Arteriography: Large uterine arteriovenous formation.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:** Persistent uterine bleeding prompted consideration of conditions like retained products of conception, gestational trophoblastic disease, endometrial pathology, or arteriovenous malformation (AVM).
   - Retained products of conception were considered due to elevated beta-HCG and ultrasound findings but did not account for the vascularized structures and velocities seen on Doppler.
   - Gestational trophoblastic disease was ruled out as beta-HCG levels were declining, and MRI findings pointed toward retained tissue and AVM instead of invasive disease.
   - Endometrial pathology was unlikely as no abnormal endometrial findings or distinct lesions were seen on examination.

2. **Role of Imaging:**
   - **Doppler Ultrasound and MRI:** Confirmed features consistent with high-velocity vascular malformations and retained trophoblastic tissue on the cesarean scar.
   - Uterine AVM was strongly suspected based on the Doppler findings of high systolic and diastolic velocities, which are classic for AVMs.

3. **Arteriography Confirmation:** Definitively diagnosed the uterine AVM and identified feeding vessels for subsequent management.

4. **Ruling Out Other Diagnoses:**
   - No evidence of external pelvic vascular malformations or tumors.
   - Infection or necrotic tissue secondary to trauma or another surgical complication did not explain the vascular findings.

5. **Management Strategy:** After confirming uterine AVM, embolization was recommended to preserve fertility, followed by operative hysteroscopy to remove necrotic retained tissue.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Acquired uterine arteriovenous malformation (AVM) associated with trophoblastic retention at the site of a prior cesarean scar.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Acquired uterine arteriovenous malformation (AVM) associated with trophoblastic retention.

results.content:
### Reasoning:

First, I need to understand the patient's current presentation and history. The patient is a 32-year-old female, gravida 2, para 1, who had a spontaneous miscarriage two months ago. She is experiencing persistent low-grade metrorrhagia since then. Her initial plasma beta-HCG level was 13,500, which suggests a significant pregnancy-related issue. She was referred for further management due to suspected trophoblastic retention.

Given that she had a cesarean section three years ago due to acute fetal distress, this history is important because cesarean sections can sometimes lead to complications such as uterine scars, which might be relevant in her current condition.

During the physical examination, she appears in good general condition, is apyretic, and hemodynamically stable with no signs of anemia. The cervix is healthy with no vaginal lesions or active bleeding from the endometrial source. This suggests that the bleeding is not from an obvious cervical or vaginal source but likely from the uterus.

The ancillary tests provide more insights:

1. **Plasma beta-HCG:** It's positive at 7100 mIU/mL, which is slightly reduced from the prior level of 13,500 mIU/mL. This indicates that there is still some trophoblastic activity present, but it's decreasing over time.

2. **Hemoglobin:** 12.1 g/dL, which is within the normal range, suggesting that she hasn't lost a significant amount of blood, at least not acutely.

3. **Ultrasound and Doppler:** There's a heterogeneous vascularized image at the anterior isthmus over the cesarean scar, with high-velocity dilated vessels. This could indicate aberrant vascularization in the area of the cesarean scar.

4. **MRI:** Shows retention measuring 30 mm associated with a uterine arteriovenous malformation (AVM).

5. **Arteriography:** Reveals a large uterine arteriovenous formation.

Putting all these findings together, it seems that the patient has a retained products of conception (likely trophoblastic tissue) in the uterus, possibly associated with an abnormal vascular structure due to the cesarean scar.

The persistence of bleeding and the presence of beta-HCG suggest that there is residual tissue causing continued trophoblastic activity. The ultrasound and MRI findings point towards a mass at the cesarean scar site, which is vascular and possibly an arteriovenous malformation.

I need to consider the differential diagnoses for persistent bleeding post-miscarriage, which include:

- Incomplete evacuation of the uterus with retained products.

- Trophoblastic disease, such as partial or complete mole.

- Infection.

- Uterine anomalies, including scar defects.

Given the presence of a cesarean scar and the imaging findings, a cesarean scar pregnancy or a retention with associated vascular abnormality seems plausible.

However, the initial miscarriage was spontaneous, and the beta-HCG levels, while high, are decreasing, suggesting that it's not a molar pregnancy. If it were a molar pregnancy, the HCG levels would typically be much higher and might not decrease as expected.

The ultrasound and MRI findings of a heterogeneous vascularized mass at the cesarean scar site, along with the arteriography showing a large arteriovenous formation, suggest that there is an abnormal vascular connection in that area.

This could be a result of the cesarean scar leading to the development of an arteriovenous fistula or a malformation, which is now associated with retained trophoblastic tissue causing persistent bleeding.

I need to consider the management options for this patient. Given the persistent bleeding and the presence of retained tissue with an associated vascular anomaly, the standard approach would be to perform a surgical intervention to remove the retained products and address the vascular abnormality to prevent further bleeding or potential complications like hemorrhage.

Alternatively, in some cases, medical management with methotrexate could be considered for trophoblastic retention, but given the presence of an AVM and the risk of bleeding, surgery might be more appropriate.

I should also consider the patient's future fertility plans, as aggressive surgery could potentially affect her uterine integrity.

In summary, the patient has persistent bleeding post-miscarriage due to retained trophoblastic tissue associated with a uterine arteriovenous malformation at the cesarean scar site.

### Answer:

Cesarean Scar Pregnancy with associated Uterine Arteriovenous Malformation and Retained Products of Conception
